input.background: A jury having heard the evidence in a criminal case, is debating whether to render a verdict of guilty or not guilty. Because it is worse to convict an innocent person of a crime they did not commit than let a guilty person go free, each case starts with a presumption of innocence. This is analogous to the presumption that $H_0$ is true in a statistical hypothesis test. Other analogies exist.
input.question: To make these connections, fill in the cells of the following table with the labels: (a) Acquittal of an innocent person; (b) Conviction of an innocent person; (c) Acquittal of a guilty person; (d) Declaring a criminal guilty.

output.answer: \begin{tabular}{r|c|c|}\multicolumn{2}{c}{ TRUTH } \DECISION OF JURY & \multicolumn{1}{c|}{ Did not do crime } & Did crime \Not Guilty & (a) & (c) \Guilty & (b) & (d) \\ \hline\end{tabular}

Verdict.description: Possible outcomes of a jury's decision in a criminal case.

Hypothesis_Test_Outcome.description: Possible outcomes of a statistical hypothesis test.
